findings.title: Start at 50/50 split as baseline; negotiate higher % as skills improve
findings.claim: 50/50 is the standard baseline commission split for new OFM agencies, with room to take a higher percentage as expertise and results grow.
findings.what_they_do: Agency locks in 50/50 as the minimum split with every creator. As the agency proves its value and techniques mature, it renegotiates upward. No tiered structure described — purely experience-based progression.
findings.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per-creator at onboarding (§7) but has no documented split rationale, tiers, or progression logic. The baseline number Palm uses is not written down. This is flagged as §7's #1 gap.
findings.recommendation: Codify 50/50 as Palm's stated baseline in the onboarding contract and pitch deck. Document criteria for when Palm negotiates above 50% (e.g., creator already earning >$X/mo, Palm providing full chatting, paid traffic spend). Gives sales clarity and creator trust.

sources.quote: you say let's partner up, I'll take 50%, you take 50%, this is the benchmark, this is the baseline

findings.topic: Cold outreach via Instagram personal brand
findings.title: Build a professional IG persona to run cold outreach to creators
findings.claim: Setting up a credible Instagram profile with professional photos (not casual/topless) is the primary vehicle for cold-outreaching creators, and showing your face builds trust that converts to signed deals.
findings.what_they_do: Agency owner creates a personal IG account with polished location/lifestyle photos (real or Pinterest-sourced). Shows face for credibility; faceless is acceptable if IG photos are professional. DMs creators who appear stuck, plateaued, or post-bad-agency. Offers a free 1-week trial to prove value, then converts to 50/50. Moves promising leads to FaceTime to close.
findings.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led outbound (§6) but no documented IG persona setup, photo standard, or structured funnel from IG profile → DM → FaceTime → sign. The free-trial-then-sign tactic is not in Palm's playbook.
findings.recommendation: Document a Palm acquisition IG profile standard: photo style, bio copy, niche positioning. Add a free-trial offer as a low-friction entry for creators already earning but skeptical. Build a short outreach sequence: IG DM → value hook → FaceTime close → contract. Cheap to implement, directly addresses §6 gap.

sources.quote: you want to just make an Instagram profile, you want to set it up some nice business photos
you can offer if you find a model and you believe in yourself… I'll work for free for you for a week and if things improve then we can go to a 50/50 split

findings.title: Target creators who are plateaued, overwhelmed, or burned by a bad agency
findings.claim: The highest-converting creator prospects are those who are stuck at a plateau, overwhelmed by workload, or have had a bad agency experience — not cold beginners.
findings.what_they_do: Agency explicitly looks for creators who: (a) plateaued after initial growth, (b) are overwhelmed managing content + chatting alone, (c) had a bad prior agency and are open to a better partner. Uses these pain points as the outreach hook.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented ICP (ideal creator profile) or qualification criteria for outbound (§6). Josh-led DM outreach is ad hoc. Targeting burned/plateaued creators is a concrete, testable filter Palm could apply immediately.
findings.recommendation: Define Palm's creator ICP in writing: earning $1K–$10K/mo, plateaued 60+ days, active IG but not growing, or recently left an agency. Use these as DM targeting filters on IG/TikTok. Add a qualification question to the onboarding form: 'Have you worked with an agency before? What happened?'

sources.quote: you're looking for models that are a bit overwhelmed, they're a bit stuck, their accounts are being plateaued, maybe they've had a bad agency before

findings.title: Google Form onboarding captures creator profile, device specs, goals, and personal traits
findings.claim: A structured onboarding form (Google Forms) that captures age, location, device model, personal traits, and goals is the operational foundation before any work begins.
findings.what_they_do: Agency sends a Google Form at signing. Fields include: age, country, iPhone model (iPhone 13+ required for IG quality standard), personal traits, content style preferences, and creator goals. Login to OF account follows, then profile audit, promotions review, and a written growth plan.
findings.vs_us: Palm has an onboarding form wizard (name, email, commission %, state, contract, voice memo — §6) but no documented device requirement, content style intake, or goal-setting fields. The iPhone 13+ quality gate is not in Palm's onboarding.
findings.recommendation: Extend Palm's onboarding wizard to include: (1) device model field with a minimum spec gate (iPhone 13+ or Android equivalent), (2) content style/persona fields, (3) creator goals (revenue target, posting comfort level, explicit limits). Add an OF profile audit step as a formal onboarding milestone before first post.

sources.quote: get up a Google Forms, fill it out — you want to know when, how old the model is, where the model's from, if the model's got an iPhone, what iPhone she's recording off
we always do iPhone 13 or above, they have to be, because Instagram care a lot about quality

findings.title: Build a linear pre-made script ladder: message → soft image → message → explicit PPV
findings.claim: A pre-made DM script that sequences plain messages, half-nude images, and full-nude PPV in a deliberate ladder is the core chatting monetization engine.
findings.what_they_do: Agency writes a reusable script sequence: opener message → follow-up message → half-nude image send → bridging message → full explicit PPV offer → continuation. Recommends doing 6–7 hours of chatting personally first to learn the craft before hiring chatters. Treats this as the primary revenue driver (80–90% of OF revenue).
findings.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced to an external team today (§8). No internal script, ladder structure, or SOP exists. This is directly relevant to Palm's roadmap item of building an in-house chat team (~1yr out) and the active AI chatting project. Frame as build-toward intelligence.
findings.recommendation: Begin drafting Palm's first pre-made script ladder now as a build-toward asset: (1) map a 5–7 step message sequence per creator persona, (2) define PPV price points per step (ties to §7 pricing gap), (3) document it in Airtable as a 'Chat Scripts' table linked to each creator. This becomes the training baseline when in-house chatting launches.

sources.quote: you want to make one message, then another, then maybe send her a half nude image, then another message, then a full nude, then blah blah blah
80 to 90% is made through chatting — the models, they're busy creating content, so they don't actually have time to do all the chatting

findings.title: Proactively coach creators on IG explicit-content ban risk to prevent account loss
findings.claim: Many creators post explicit content on IG out of ignorance, and the agency's value-add is intervening before the ban happens, not after.
findings.what_they_do: Agency flags explicit-content posting as a ban trigger and actively tells creators to stop. Positions this knowledge as a differentiator when pitching creators — 'I can protect your account' is part of the acquisition hook. General IG compliance coaching (what to post, how often, which platforms) is part of the agency's ongoing service.
findings.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is data-driven for ideation but has no documented compliance coaching SOP for creators around IG content rules. Real-creator IG warmup/cadence is also flagged as a gap (§4 THIN for real creators).
findings.recommendation: Add a 'Platform Rules' module to Palm's creator onboarding: a short brief covering IG explicit-content policy, shadowban triggers, and safe posting formats. Deliver this in the first week. Also use IG compliance knowledge as a sales proof point in acquisition outreach ('we've kept X accounts safe').

sources.quote: some will do really explicit content on IG and we all know now that is not a thing you can do — if you do put this content up on IG your account's going to get banned

findings.title: Run weekly payouts and performance check-ins as a retention and motivation tool
findings.claim: Weekly payouts ('weekly pouch') keep both the agency and creator motivated and signal operational professionalism early in the relationship.
findings.what_they_do: After onboarding, agency moves to a weekly payout cadence. Framed as both a financial rhythm and a morale tool — seeing money arrive weekly reinforces creator buy-in and agency credibility. No detailed reporting format described, but the weekly cadence is presented as standard practice.
findings.vs_us: Palm auto-computes weekly invoices (§7) so the cadence matches. However, Palm has no documented creator-facing performance review or check-in tied to the payout — it's finance-only. No retention/success cadence is written down (§9 GAP).
findings.recommendation: Attach a lightweight weekly creator report to Palm's existing invoice email: top 3 metrics (subscribers, revenue, top-performing post). Takes minimal effort given Palm's Airtable data and Resend pipeline, and transforms a finance touchpoint into a retention and upsell moment.

sources.quote: after a week or two, we do weekly pouch, just to let you know — that's just a cool little tip, enjoy the money coming in

findings.title: Free teaser → $5–15 qualifier → $30–40+ escalation PPV ladder
findings.claim: Starting PPV with a free non-revealing image, then a small $5–15 paid image, then escalating to $30–40+ video creates a progressive upsell funnel that qualifies spenders before committing to expensive content.
findings.what_they_do: Chatter sends (1) a free, non-revealing image to open the conversation; (2) a slightly more revealing image/clip priced $5–15 to qualify whether the fan will spend; (3) subsequent escalating PPV pieces priced $30–40+. Welcome/intro message is auto-sent at $15–25 on subscribe. Each step is treated as a 'drink at the bar' — the fan self-selects into a higher spend tier organically.
findings.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is outsourced to an external chatting team (§8). No internal PPV ladder SOP exists. This is a concrete, step-by-step PPV sequencing framework Palm's future in-house chat team or AI chatting project will need.
findings.recommendation: Document this exact 3-step ladder (free teaser → $5–15 qualifier → $30–40+ escalation) as a foundational SOP for the in-house chat roadmap. Set up the auto-send intro PPV at $15–25 on subscribe as a baseline for every new subscriber across all creators.

sources.quote: the next video or picture will be a little more revealing you'll sell that for five to ten dollars and so on and so forth the next one from there will be 30 to 40
the first ppv messages should be pictures that are like five to ten dollars you're just trying to qualify if this guy is a spender or a Time waster

findings.topic: Price integrity / repackaging instead of discounting
findings.title: Never fold on PPV price — repackage content instead of discounting
findings.claim: When a fan doesn't purchase at the stated PPV price, cutting the price cheapens the brand; instead, trim or repackage the same video at a lower price point so it appears to be different content.
findings.what_they_do: If a fan ignores or declines a PPV, the chatter does not reduce the listed price. Instead, they clip the same video to a different length, change whether a thumbnail is shown, or rebundle it, then re-pitch it as a 'different video you might like' at a lower price. This preserves the perceived value of the original piece.
findings.vs_us: No internal PPV pricing model or price-integrity policy is documented at Palm (§7 GAP). This is a concrete tactic for the in-house chat and pricing buildout.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'no-discount, repackage only' rule to the future chat team SOP. Build a small asset library of clip variants (different lengths, thumbnail on/off) for each PPV set so chatters have ready-made repackaged versions to offer.

sources.quote: if there's a point where they don't purchase don't fold on your pricing what you can do though is you can trim videos make them different lengths repackage it so it doesn't seem like you are folding

findings.topic: Inbound-pull DM strategy vs. mass PPV blasting
findings.title: Use feed posts as DM bait to pull fans inbound rather than blasting PPV
findings.claim: Posting a simple 'I'm online / bored' caption on the creator's feed draws fans into DMs on their own initiative, giving the chatter frame control and avoiding the 'spammy' feel of mass PPV messages.
findings.what_they_do: Instead of mass-sending PPV blasts, the chatter (or creator post) publishes a low-key feed post ('hey I'm up, who's up?', 'come to my DMs right now') that signals availability without directly soliciting money. Fans who respond have self-selected as engaged; the chatter is now in a reactive, high-authority position to run the upsell sequence.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented DM-pull strategy or mass-message cadence guidance (§8 outsourced, §5 GAP on traffic/distribution strategy). This inbound-pull framing is a complementary tactic to whatever the external chat team is doing and is relevant for the in-house roadmap.
findings.recommendation: Incorporate 'availability bait' posts as a scheduled content type in the Publer/Airtable pipeline — short captions designed to trigger DM inflow, not tied to a content asset. Brief the external chat team on this so they're primed when the flood comes, and document it as a standard practice for the in-house chat SOP.

sources.quote: post on your page say hey I'm online hey I'm bored um just post a picture caption it and they'll get the idea that you're online
you don't want to ever spam with ppv this is a huge No-No it cheapens your brand

findings.title: Log personal fan details in notes so any chatter can continue the relationship seamlessly
findings.claim: Chatters add personal details (job, location, interests) to each fan's notes or rename field so any chatter who picks up the conversation later can maintain the illusion of a continuous, invested relationship.
findings.what_they_do: After a fan shares personal information in DMs, the chatter immediately records it in that fan's notes inside the OF messaging interface (or an external CRM). The fan's name field may be renamed to include key tags. When a different chatter takes over a shift, they review notes first and reference those personal details naturally — the fan feels remembered and valued, which increases LTV.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting tools (§9), but no written retention/chatter handoff SOP. The notes-per-fan mechanic described here is a direct operational input for the Fan Tracker and for the in-house chat team SOP.
findings.recommendation: Formalise a 'fan card' field set in the Fan Tracker (or OF notes) — at minimum: spending tier, personal details shared, last PPV purchased, last contact date. Require the external chat team to populate this today; make it mandatory in the in-house chat SOP. Aligns with and extends §9 whale-hunting data.

sources.quote: we'll have our chatting team add notes to each fan where you can write down personable information and the next chatter that hops on to talk to that dude will kind of have an idea
maybe he's told the girl what he does for a living where he lives and we'll put all that information in his notes or in his name

findings.title: Configure an auto-sent paid intro message ($15–25) to fire on every new subscriber
findings.claim: Setting up an automated PPV intro message that triggers the moment a fan subscribes generates passive revenue from every new sub with zero chatter effort.
findings.what_they_do: Inside OF settings, a welcome message is pre-loaded with a PPV attachment priced at $15–25. It fires automatically to every new subscriber. No chatter action required. This is the first monetization touchpoint before any live chat begins, and it also flags whether the new sub is a likely spender (purchase = qualified lead for the upsell ladder).
findings.vs_us: No Palm-documented equivalent (§8 outsourced, §7 GAP on PPV setup). This is a zero-marginal-effort revenue lever that the external chat team can implement immediately for every Palm creator, and should be baked into the in-house chat onboarding checklist.
findings.recommendation: Immediately brief the external chat team to verify every Palm creator has an auto-send intro PPV configured at $15–25. Make this a checklist item in creator onboarding (§6 form wizard). Track intro-PPV purchase rate per creator as a leading indicator of subscriber quality.

sources.quote: another way that you can quickly gauge how interested a fan is and your content is if they purchase your intro message this is a message that you set up to automatically send out once they hit subscribe
usually our welcome messages are like 15 to 25 bucks but then the first ppv we send is like 5 to 15

findings.topic: Revenue split between subscriptions vs. DMs
findings.title: Benchmark: most OF revenue comes from messages, not subscriptions
findings.claim: The vast majority of a high-performing creator's OF income comes from DM/PPV revenue, not subscription fees — illustrated by a creator earning $68K from subs vs. $300K+ from messages.
findings.what_they_do: Black Cherry uses this ratio as a core KPI framing: subscriptions are the door, DM monetization is the business. They orient their entire agency operation (chatters, scripts, PPV strategy) around maximising message revenue rather than subscriber count.
findings.vs_us: Palm's invoicing system tracks total creator revenue and commissions (§7) but no documented KPI split between subscription vs. message revenue exists. Palm's content engine (§1) is optimised for top-of-funnel subscriber acquisition; the DM monetisation side is outsourced with limited visibility (§8).
findings.recommendation: Add a sub-vs-message revenue split metric to Palm's invoicing dashboard and whale-hunting reports (§9). Use this ratio as a health KPI per creator: if message revenue is <50% of total, the chatting strategy is underperforming. Share this benchmark with the external chat team as an accountability target.

sources.quote: only made 68 000 over 300 000 literally from the subscriptions the only fan subscriptions and most of the money as you can see came from the messages themselves

findings.title: Train chatters with agency-owned PPV scripts rather than relying on their defaults
findings.claim: Not all chatters come with polished PPV skills; agencies that hand chatters specific scripts and strategies outperform those that leave chatters to their own methods.
findings.what_they_do: Black Cherry operates a chatting team internally but also licenses their PPV script framework to other agency operators. They provide PDF script playbooks, documented PPV funnels, and course-based training covering at least seven distinct PPV strategies. The agency owns the IP; chatters execute to spec.
findings.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced with no internal scripts or SOPs (§8). When Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap), it will need proprietary script libraries and training material. This competitor model — agency owns the playbook, chatters execute — is the architecture Palm should adopt.
findings.recommendation: Begin building Palm's internal PPV script library now, drawing from competitor frameworks like this one. Structure it as: (1) welcome/intro message template, (2) conversation bait posts, (3) upsell ladder scripts per price tier, (4) objection-handling / repackaging scripts. This becomes the training corpus for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project.

sources.quote: you need to know these strategies so that you can train shatters to be this good not all Chatters have mastered the game they know what they know but when you can give them scripts like this
Down Below there's actually going to be a PDF attached where you can see a very precise example of how we run this

findings.title: Hierarchical folder labeling system for OF vault content reuse
findings.claim: Organizing vault content by year/quarter/month/week with descriptive labels enables fast retrieval and reselling to new subscribers.
findings.what_they_do: Creator maintains a Dropbox folder tree (Year → Q1-Q4 → Month → Week → Day), names individual files descriptively (e.g. 'pink outfit 1', 'pink outfit 2'), and mirrors this structure inside the OF vault using OF's native label/folder feature. Only edited, reusable content is saved. Legacy content is batched into a single 'pre-2022' folder and backfilled gradually rather than all at once.
findings.vs_us: Palm's content system (§2) is THIN on OF vault/PPV organization. The social pipeline (§1) is mature for top-of-funnel assets but there is no documented system for tagging, retrieving, or reselling vault content. This is a direct gap in the OF vault layer.
findings.recommendation: Define a vault taxonomy standard for Palm-managed creators: Year → Month → content-type label (e.g. '2024-03-booty', '2024-03-face'). Add a vault-labeling step to the existing editor workflow so every uploaded asset is tagged before the task is closed. Build a lightweight 'resell menu' Airtable view that surfaces labeled vault assets by creator for chatter/PPV use.

sources.quote: I took everything from before 2022 and I put that in one folder and now I created a new folder and it is called of content 2023 and from there I have q1 2 3 and 4

findings.title: Pin a pre-made content menu to OF page to drive passive vault resales
findings.claim: Pinning a priced menu of previously sent content to the OF page lets new subscribers self-serve old vault content without creator intervention.
findings.what_they_do: Creator builds a text-based menu listing past content items with descriptions and prices, pins it to their OF page. New subscribers browse it and request items by name; creator pulls from the vault folder and sends. No active selling required — the menu does the work.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented equivalent for a passive vault-resell menu (§2 gap). The chatting function is outsourced (§8), so the external chat team likely has no visibility into a structured content menu either. This is a low-effort monetization layer currently missing.
findings.recommendation: Standardize a pinned content menu for each Palm creator. The editor or ops team maintains it in Airtable (listing vault item name, label, price, thumbnail). Brief the external chat team to reference this menu when fans ask about past content. When the in-house chat team is built (~1yr roadmap), make menu-referencing a core chat SOP.

sources.quote: I also created another menu that has previously sent out content so I've just organized it I label what it is and the price and then I've also pinned that to my page

findings.title: Length-based PPV pricing ladder: $5–$8 short, $8–$20 long, nothing under 1 min
findings.claim: Pricing PPV content by video length with a hard floor of 1 minute and a practical ceiling of ~$20 maximizes purchase rate across different fan spending levels.
findings.what_they_do: Short or clothed videos: under $8. Longer or more explicit videos: $8–$20. Hard rule: never charge for content under 1 minute — that goes free. Photos posted free to subscribers (subscription covers them); photos only charged if custom. Sends a mix of price points so lower-budget fans still convert on cheaper items while high-spenders can hit the $20–$25 ceiling.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model (§7 — #1 gap flagged). No internal baseline exists for price ladders, anchoring, or length-based rules. This is pure learn-from-others territory.
findings.recommendation: Adopt a default PPV pricing framework for all Palm creators: <1 min = free/engagement only; 1–3 min = $5–$8; 3–8 min = $8–$15; 8+ min = $15–$25. Document as a pricing SOP and share with the external chat team so they apply consistent pricing in DMs. Review per-creator after 30 days and adjust based on conversion data already in the system.

sources.quote: if I send out a shorter video or like a twerking video or I'm clothed then it's somewhere under like eight dollars and if it's a little bit more than that then it's somewhere over eight dollars always under I would say twenty dollars

findings.title: Send at least one PPV mass message per day — treat it like TV advertising repetition
findings.claim: Sending PPV messages at minimum once daily, using a TV-advertising repetition mindset, is the single biggest lever for DM revenue most creators underuse.
findings.what_they_do: Creator sends PPV mass messages at least once per day, sometimes multiple times. Frames it explicitly as a repetition/awareness play (Tide detergent analogy): fans who don't buy message #1 may buy message #5. Explicitly rejects the 'one message per week' approach. Pairs this with multiple free posts per day on the OF feed to maintain presence.
findings.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) and there is no documented PPV cadence SOP. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap) and the AI chatting project. The daily-minimum rule is a concrete, codifiable KPI.
findings.recommendation: Add a PPV send-frequency KPI to the external chat team's performance brief immediately: minimum 1 mass PPV per creator per day. When building the in-house chat SOP (roadmap), hard-code this as a daily task in the chatter checklist. For the AI chatting project, model automated PPV scheduling at 1× daily with A/B price variation.

sources.quote: you should be sending out pay-per-view messages at least once a day if you're not you are probably missing out on a lot of money

findings.title: Batch shoot 5 looks → batch schedule 5 days of page posts + paired PPV messages
findings.claim: Batching a multi-look shoot and scheduling all resulting page posts and PPV messages in one session prevents daily scramble and ensures consistent output.
findings.what_they_do: In a single shoot session: 5 outfits/looks, each producing ~3 photos + 2 videos. Immediately after editing, schedules each outfit's photos to post on consecutive days AND schedules the corresponding PPV video message to go out on the same day as those photos. Result: 5 days of content planned in one sitting. Uses OF's native calendar scheduler.
findings.vs_us: Palm's social content pipeline (§1) is strong on ideation and editing but posting cadence for real creators is not codified (§1 🔲 GAP). The OF vault/PPV side (§2) has no batch-scheduling SOP. This tactic bridges both gaps with a concrete daily-output model.
findings.recommendation: Define a batch content session standard for Palm real creators: minimum 3 looks per session, each look producing 2–3 photos (page) + 1 video (PPV). Ops team schedules page posts and paired PPV messages together in one Publer/OF scheduling pass. Add a 'batch session' record type to the Airtable Content Ops base to track sessions, looks, scheduled dates, and PPV prices per asset.

sources.quote: say I did five different looks today I went and did my photo shoot five different looks each look I took three photos and two videos so then what I would do is I would schedule you know tomorrow these photos the next day these photos

findings.topic: subscriber list segmentation for PPV targeting
findings.title: Segment subscriber lists by content preference and renewal status to send targeted PPV messages
findings.claim: Using OF's native list feature to segment fans by content preference (feet, striptease, etc.) and renewal status enables personalized PPV sends that feel direct and increase conversion.
findings.what_they_do: Creates named lists inside OF (beyond defaults): VIP list for high-paying subscribers ($200/mo tier who get PPV free), preference-based lists (feet fans, striptease fans, etc.). When sending a PPV mass message, selects the relevant list as the target or excludes VIP list from paid messages. Sends preference-matched messages to niche lists with personalized copy to increase perceived intimacy.
findings.vs_us: Palm's fan intelligence layer (§9) has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting module but no documented PPV targeting by content preference. Chatting is outsourced (§8) so the external team may not be segmenting sends. This is build-toward for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project.
findings.recommendation: Brief the external chat team to begin using OF list segmentation immediately: at minimum, maintain a 'VIP/renewing' list excluded from paid PPV, and build 2–3 preference lists per creator based on DM history. Feed preference data back into the Fan Tracker CRM (§9). When building the in-house chat SOP, make list maintenance a weekly chatter task. For the AI chatting project, model preference tagging as a fan-profile attribute driving automated PPV selection.

sources.quote: you can also create lists for certain people like if you know there are certain people that really like feet pictures or there's certain group of people that really like striptease videos you can add them to that list and then whenever you do want to send out a certain type of message you can send it to everybody

findings.topic: objection and boundary handling in DMs
findings.title: Two-warning boundary script: redirect → stern warning → block to protect creator brand
findings.claim: A scripted two-step response to out-of-scope requests (redirect to alternative, then firm final warning before block) maintains creator boundaries without losing a potential buyer.
findings.what_they_do: Step 1: When fan requests something creator doesn't offer, redirect: 'I don't do that but I do [X] — can I get you a custom video?' Step 2: If fan is pushy after first redirect, deliver a firm second message: 'I've told you once, I'll tell you twice — I'll block you if you continue.' Step 3: Block. Never debates or apologizes. Frames the redirect as a sales opportunity, not a refusal.
findings.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) with no internal scripts or objection-handling SOPs. This is concrete build-toward material for the in-house chat SOP. The redirect-to-upsell framing is especially valuable — it turns a boundary scenario into a custom content sale.
findings.recommendation: Add this two-step boundary script to the future in-house chat team's SOP as a default response template. Share with the external chat team now as a briefing document. For the AI chatting project, model this as a conditional response tree: out-of-scope request → redirect + custom upsell offer → if repeated → block flag for human review.

sources.quote: if someone's asking for something that I don't do I just say hey I don't actually do that but I do blank if you want to get a custom video or a custom picture whatever it is and I kind of offer them an alternative

findings.title: Personalized openers using username or profile detail to force engagement
findings.claim: Sending a message personalized to a specific subscriber's username or visible profile detail dramatically increases response rates versus generic openers.
findings.what_they_do: Chatters are required to craft a unique opener for each sub that contains a play on the subscriber's username (e.g., 'Hey Jimmy, are you the same Jimmy Johnson from the Dallas Cowboys?'). The opener must include exactly one question — no more — so it opens a single conversational thread the sub feels obligated to answer. Copy-paste openers like 'hey how are you' or 'thanks for the follow' are explicitly banned. The opener is designed to (a) prove a human is typing, (b) put the chatter in conversational control by triggering a reaction, and (c) give the sub an easy, interesting thing to respond to.
findings.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced to an external chat team (§8); there is no internal SOP or opener framework at Palm. This is pure build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat team roadmap.
findings.recommendation: When Palm builds its in-house chat team, mandate personalized username-based openers as the baseline standard. Create a training doc that (1) defines what counts as a valid personalized opener, (2) lists banned copy-paste patterns, and (3) drills the one-question rule. Include this in pre-training screening — candidates who default to 'hey how are you' should be filtered.

sources.quote: send a personalized opener that is unique to the person who is receiving it
it must contain one question but no more than one question

findings.title: Four-axis sub qualification (profession, location, age, hobbies) to size wallet and build rapport
findings.claim: Systematically qualifying subscribers on four data points — profession, location, age, and hobbies — lets chatters estimate spending capacity and tailor their communication style before attempting to sell.
findings.what_they_do: After the personalized opener gets a response, chatters move into a structured qualification sequence. They ask about: (1) Profession — men spend 40+ hrs/week on work and will talk about it freely; also signals income level. (2) Location — dictates tone, cultural references, and timezone. (3) Age — a 52-year-old has 30 more years of earning history than a 22-year-old; older subs get more formal language, younger subs get slang. (4) Hobbies — reveals personality and gives commonality-building material. Chatters are also taught 'level two qualification' — drilling one level deeper on each topic (e.g., 'How did you get into that job?') to extract more information and signal genuine interest.
findings.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm for chatter qualification frameworks (§8, outsourced). Build-toward for in-house chat team.
findings.recommendation: Build a qualification checklist into the in-house chatter training SOP: chatters must capture profession, location, age, and at least one hobby for every sub before escalating to selling. Store this data per-fan in the Fan Tracker CRM (§9) to enable personalized re-engagement. Age and profession data should also feed the whale-hunting logic — a 50-year-old professional is a higher-priority cultivation target than an 18-year-old student.

findings.topic: Commonality building as the core sales engine
findings.title: Three-tier commonality system (me-too / mutual contact / tangential connection) to manufacture emotional bond
findings.claim: Building plausible commonalities with subscribers — using a tiered system from direct 'me too' to loose tangential connections — is the single most important driver of repeat purchasing.
findings.what_they_do: After qualification, chatters build commonalities using three levels: Level 1 ('me too') — chatter claims to share the same trait directly (e.g., 'I also study psychology'). Used sparingly because it's hard to track across many subs. Level 2 ('I know someone who') — chatter links via a third party ('My little brother studies psychology'). Common but overuse feels weird. Level 3 (tangential connection) — chatter links the sub's trait to something loosely related ('I read a Harvard Business Review article about students who study under 2 hours a night…'). This is the core technique — it's harder to fake, opens new conversational threads, and doesn't require the chatter to claim personal overlap. The goal is to give a 'plausible reason' for emotional connection so that future sales requests don't feel transactional.
findings.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward. Notably, the 'plausible reason for validation' logic is essentially applied sales psychology that Palm could encode as a training principle, not just a chatting script.
findings.recommendation: Make Level 3 commonality building the centrepiece of Palm's in-house chatter training. Create a worked-example library organized by profession/hobby (e.g., 'if sub says he's a nurse, here are three Level 3 connections'). Pair this with the qualification CRM data so chatters can prep connections before re-engaging a returning sub.

sources.quote: building commonalities this is going to be the most important section in this presentation
oh really that's interesting I was reading an article in the Harvard Business review and it said that students who study under 2 hours a night do 50% worse

findings.title: Inject scripted rapport breaks and push-pull frames throughout chat to create chase dynamic
findings.claim: Deliberately breaking rapport with playful negative statements (e.g., 'I was starting to like you until you said that') and push-pull phrasing prevents the sub from losing interest and causes them to actively seek the chatter's validation — which is the precondition for purchasing.
findings.what_they_do: Chatters are drilled on two borrowed-from-pickup techniques: (1) Rapport breaks — any statement that actively discourages the deepening of connection, always softened as a joke (e.g., 'you and I would never get along because you're a math teacher and I have such trauma about math teachers'). These are not standalone — they must be followed up or contextualized so they don't read as mean over text. (2) Push-pulls — a positive statement immediately followed by a negative, or vice versa (e.g., 'you're actually kind of funny and I bet people teased you for it growing up'). These are to be peppered throughout the entire conversation, not saved for one moment. The psychological goal: sub chases chatter's validation, which increases desire to please — i.e., spend money.
findings.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward. This is a differentiating technique that separates high-performing chatters from low ones and is absent from most agency training.
findings.recommendation: Include rapport breaks and push-pull scripting as a dedicated module in Palm's in-house chatter training. Provide 10–15 templated examples per technique that chatters memorize, then practice in role-play before going live. Monitor for correct usage in chat reviews — a chatter who never uses these is likely glazing, which suppresses sales.

sources.quote: a break in rapport anything that actively discourages the deepening or establishment of a positive connection
you should be doing push pulls you should really be doing breaks and rapport or specifically push pulls throughout the entire interaction

findings.topic: PPV value ladder and first-sale anchoring
findings.title: Value ladder starting at sub-$5 to condition purchasing habit before escalating price
findings.claim: The first PPV sale should always be $5 or less to condition the subscriber to buy; subsequent sales increase in price incrementally, and the overall set is structured as 7 separate sales attempts at progressively higher price points.
findings.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to structure one content 'set' as 7 distinct sales opportunities (individual pics, pic+video combos, etc.) with prices rising at each step. The first sale is always ≤$5 — not because the content is cheap, but to make the buy frictionless and establish a Pavlovian habit: sub sees PPV → sub unlocks. Subs who won't spend $5 are deprioritized or ignored. New chatters are taught to use small price increments (e.g., $5 → $7 → $10) rather than jumping to $25+. Selling too much too soon is explicitly called out as a sub-burning mistake; longevity and lifetime value are the stated goal.
findings.vs_us: No documented PPV pricing model at Palm (§7 GAP). The '7 sales per set' structure and sub-$5 entry point are concrete, adoptable mechanics. Palm's outsourced chat team may have their own approach, but it is undocumented internally.
findings.recommendation: Adopt this ladder as Palm's default PPV pricing framework when building the in-house chat team. Codify: (1) first PPV always ≤$5, (2) 7 sales-attempt structure per set, (3) incremental price increases per sale, (4) subs who decline the entry price are de-prioritized. Feed this into the Fan Tracker (§9) so chatters can see where each fan sits on the ladder and what the next logical price point is.

findings.title: Write immersive, boner-test PPV descriptions that sell the fantasy before the sub sees the content
findings.claim: Long, vivid PPV descriptions that pass a 'can you picture it with your eyes closed' imagination test and a 'does it arouse' boner test dramatically increase unlock rates because the sub is buying stimulation from the description itself, not the preview.
findings.what_they_do: Chatters are required to write out full descriptions for every PPV — not 'here's a pic of my boobs babe' but a multi-sentence, immersive description of camera angle, positioning, what the model is doing, and what the sub should imagine. Two quality tests: (1) Imagination test — can someone visualize exactly what is in the content just from the words, with no preview? (2) Boner test — does the description itself arouse? If a male chatter reads it and finds it hot, it will likely work. Pre-writing descriptions for an entire set before the selling session begins is standard practice. This is treated as a core skill, not an afterthought.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no internal PPV copywriting standard (§2 THIN, §7 GAP). This is directly actionable: even before building an in-house chat team, Palm could create a PPV description template and writing guide for the external chat team and for creator-facing content briefing.
findings.recommendation: Create a PPV description writing guide with the two-test framework (imagination + boner test) and worked examples contrasting bad ('here's a pic babe') vs. good (fully immersive). Deliver this to the external chat team now as a quick win. When building the in-house team, make PPV description writing a standalone scored exercise in the pre-training filter — candidates who can't write a vivid description after coaching should not progress.

sources.quote: they are not vivid they do not stimulate the subscriber's imagination
does the description vividly describe what is in the content… will the description give the sub a boner when they read it

findings.title: Use narrative 'gambits' (e.g., broken phone, emergency) to collect money outside standard PPV sales
findings.claim: Chatters can collect money from subscribers through creative life-narrative requests (e.g., 'I dropped my phone, can you help?') that are emotionally compelling and do not require the sub to be in a purchasing mindset for explicit content.
findings.what_they_do: Gambits are defined as non-sexual sales techniques where the chatter creates a plausible real-life reason for the model to need money and asks the subscriber to contribute. Examples: broken phone screen, emergency expense. These are framed as personal requests, not content sales, so they bypass the sub's resistance to PPV unlocking. Key rule: gambits must not violate platform ToS (no meetup offers). The presenter treats this as an advanced technique taught in coaching rather than to new chatters, but frames it as unlocking a completely different revenue channel beyond PPV.
findings.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward. Palm should be aware this tactic exists and is widely used; understanding it is necessary for the AI chatting project to handle these flows appropriately, and for the in-house team SOP to define what gambits are permitted vs. prohibited.
findings.recommendation: Include gambits as a section in the in-house chatter SOP with: (1) a definition and worked examples, (2) a clear ToS compliance checklist (no meetup implication, no off-platform payment requests), (3) a tiered rollout — teach only to experienced chatters, not new hires. For the AI chatting roadmap, flag that gambit handling requires careful scripting to stay compliant and authentic.

you can come up with any reason for them to purchase from you to send you money any reason that you can think of that does not violate the terms

findings.title: Commission-plus-bonus comp structure with daily ranked leaderboard drives consistent chatter output
findings.claim: A commission-only base combined with milestone bonuses, daily ranked leaderboards shared with the team, and regular chat reviews sustains chatter performance over time and is the structural reason revenue grew from ~$20k/month to $80k+.
findings.what_they_do: Daily: an assistant compiles each chatter's individual revenue and ranks them on a leaderboard shared with the whole team — publicly feeding ego and competitive instinct. Comp structure: commission only plus bonuses tied to achievable milestones (the presenter spent three days designing this mathematically; result was a ~25% revenue lift). Chat reviews: manager logs into accounts periodically, reviews a sample of conversations, records a screen-capture with verbal feedback, sends to the chatter. Every chat review correlates with a numbers increase. Pre-training filter used before formal training to screen candidates — only those who pass pre-training get to the actual training stage. Hiring criteria codified into a checklist (not all personality types are suited to the role).
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented chatter hiring criteria, comp structure, or performance management cadence (§8 outsourced, §10 THIN). This is the operational skeleton Palm will need when building the in-house chat team.
findings.recommendation: When scoping the in-house chat team build (~1yr roadmap per §8): (1) Design a commission + milestone-bonus comp structure before the first hire — do not default to hourly. (2) Implement a daily revenue leaderboard visible to the whole chat team. (3) Build a pre-training filter (written test + role-play) that candidates must pass before formal onboarding. (4) Schedule weekly chat reviews for each chatter, not just when numbers drop. These four structural elements can be planned now and should be in the in-house team design spec.
